video_title: Momentum Is Crashing, Bitcoin Is Bottoming, AI Agents Are Rising
gist: Jordi Visser argues the AI-infrastructure pullback is a momentum/positioning shakeout inside a still-intact earnings-driven bull market, not a bubble bursting, while flagging application-layer names (insurance, healthcare admin, regional banks), GLM 5.2's threat to Anthropic's revenue trajectory, and a Fed (Kevin Warsh) unlikely to hike as reasons he's rotated capital into Marvell, Eli Lilly, silver, and Bitcoin, which he sees showing its first bottoming divergence.

themes.theme: Momentum crash vs. broadening bull market

insights.claim: The equal-weight S&P 500 is breaking out to new strength even as headline volatility spikes, contradicting the popular narrative that the market is a bubble about to correct.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Watch equal-weight S&P breadth, not headline index price, to judge whether the rally is broadening or narrowing before trusting bearish AI-bubble calls.
insights.quote: Equal weight S&P is breaking out and is strong despite all the volatility.

insights.claim: The Russell 2000 (IWM) made new all-time highs the same week the S&P 500 sat in a consolidation triangle, showing rotation into small caps rather than a broad market top.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Small-cap strength alongside large-cap consolidation is a rotation signal, not a risk-off signal.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 has been essentially flat since early May through June, a sideways consolidation that is 'taking air out of the bubble' while remaining an earnings-driven bull market.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Time-based consolidation without a price collapse is bullish digestion, not distribution, in this framework.

insights.claim: NYSE cumulative breadth hit an all-time high the same week the S&P index itself was merely consolidating, signaling improving participation beneath the surface.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Rising cumulative breadth during an index consolidation argues against a distribution-top thesis.

insights.claim: The four largest hyperscalers (Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Google) are roughly unchanged in price versus a year ago despite growing earnings, meaning they are undergoing multiple compression similar to what happened to SaaS software stocks.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Hyperscaler weakness is a valuation reset, not an earnings problem, so he still favors them structurally as the 'funding side' of the AI trade.

insights.claim: A healthy AI-infrastructure consolidation looks like a 50-day moving-average retracement (via time, price, or both) that washes out margin/leveraged traders without a bubble-style collapse.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Use a 50-day MA retest as the technical marker for a constructive, buyable consolidation versus a genuine breakdown.

insights.claim: Momentum-factor volatility (Morgan Stanley momentum and tech momentum indices) is now swinging outside its normal 5% daily band more violently than during the dot-com bubble, a sign of crowding rather than a reason to short the AI trade.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Extreme momentum vol is a positioning/crowding shakeout mechanism (stopping out hedge funds and retail breakout-chasers), not new fundamental information.

insights.claim: 2026 has been the worst start to the year for the momentum factor in five years, consistent with a seasonal pattern where June and December are historically weak months for momentum.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Seasonal momentum weakness argues the current shakeout may be partly calendar-driven and could ease outside these historically bad windows.

insights.claim: Growing use of AI trading agents by individual investors, particularly in Asia, will structurally increase volatility going forward, but this is bullish for markets since agents optimize purely for absolute returns rather than Sharpe ratio or drawdown limits like hedge funds do.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Rising vol from AI-agent-driven retail flow should not be read as a bearish signal; the players most at risk are drawdown-constrained hedge funds, not the market itself.

insights.claim: Margin debt is rising but remains below the midpoint of its post-Great-Financial-Crisis range when measured relative to total US market capitalization, undercutting the popular 'rising margin debt = bubble' chart.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Discount margin-debt-level bubble warnings unless they are scaled to market cap, the same way home debt should be scaled to home values.

insights.claim: US corporate earnings estimate revisions remain net positive and Europe just posted its biggest positive revision reading in two years, while global revisions sit near cycle highs — the real warning sign to watch for is when revisions broadly turn negative.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Track the balance of upward vs. downward estimate revisions as the leading indicator for when this bull market actually turns, rather than price action alone.

insights.claim: Aggregate weekly payrolls (hours worked times wages times headcount) remain flat at levels last seen from the post-GFC period through COVID, showing no labor-market acceleration despite the AI boom narrative, with healthcare dominating the monthly job gains.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Weak aggregate payrolls growth argues against the 'tight labor market / wage inflation' case some macro commentators use to justify Fed hawkishness.

insights.claim: Market breadth confirmed the improving picture: about 8% of S&P 500 constituents (roughly 40 names) hit 52-week highs versus only one new low, with 13 of those 40 in financials and 9 in healthcare.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: New-high leadership concentrated in financials and healthcare points to where the next leg of the rotation trade may be strongest.

themes.theme: AI infrastructure headwinds, open-source competition, and the Anthropic risk

insights.claim: The current AI-infrastructure pullback is driven by real headwinds — data-center power and cooling constraints, memory bottlenecks, and regulation — but these represent turbulence within an earnings-driven trade, not the start of a bubble collapse.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Distinguish 'headwind' (slows the plane) from 'stall' (crashes the plane) when evaluating each new negative AI headline.
insights.quote: Headwinds don't stop things. Planes don't hit a headwind and then go straight down. You get turbulence, and that's what we're going through at this point.

insights.claim: Gavin Baker's 'composable models' framework holds that frontier closed models will keep pricing power for the highest-stakes decisions (drug discovery, science, energy), while open-source models absorb most routine workflow tasks — frontier tokens capture about 90% of economic value even though open-source tokens make up roughly 80%+ of total tokens processed.
insights.speaker: Gavin Baker (quoted by host)
insights.so_what: Don't treat open-source model adoption as a zero-sum threat to frontier-model revenue; value capture and token-volume share are decoupling.

insights.claim: GLM 5.2, an open-weight Chinese model, is being called by multiple credible voices (Gavin Baker, Marc Andreessen, the head of Databricks) the first Chinese model to match or beat top US frontier models, and it forced Gavin Baker to rethink part of his own framework on whether open source could keep pace.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: GLM 5.2's capability jump is a genuine data point for reassessing the open-source-vs-frontier competitive gap, not noise.

insights.claim: If GLM-5.2-class open models materially erode enterprise demand for closed frontier APIs, it creates a real risk to Anthropic's revenue growth trajectory, since the market has been pricing Anthropic on an assumed continued ~10x annual revenue multiple implying roughly a trillion-dollar valuation by the end of next year.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Watch Anthropic's ARR growth rate for signs it is decelerating below the ~10x pace the market has implicitly priced in; a miss would likely become a major bearish story.

insights.claim: Dean Ball, a former Trump administration AI official recently hired by OpenAI, argues in a new paper that current US AI regulatory uncertainty creates a 'dangerous limbo' that lets open-source and Chinese models catch up and makes other countries hesitant to standardize on US models.
insights.speaker: Dean Ball (via host)
insights.so_what: US AI regulatory ambiguity is itself a competitive headwind versus Chinese open-source models, independent of raw model capability.

insights.claim: JPMorgan now views a cyberattack-triggered banking crisis as more likely than one caused by credit losses, and Jordi frames autonomous AI agent swarms as a bigger systemic risk to the fiat financial system than quantum computing poses to Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Treat agentic-AI-enabled cyber risk to banking infrastructure as an underpriced tail risk relative to the more commonly discussed quantum-vs-Bitcoin risk.
insights.quote: Agent swarms are bigger risk to the fiat system than quantum is to Bitcoin.

insights.claim: A reported AI architecture breakthrough in memory efficiency (highlighted by Andrew Curran, from a team spun out rather than a major lab) should not trigger selling memory stocks, since no details exist yet on implementation timeline or scope, and memory remains structurally scarce.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Wait for implementation details before repricing memory-stock exposure off of a single viral architecture paper.

insights.claim: After capturing roughly a 7-10x gain in a stock like Micron, an investor should expect at most a double (roughly 100-200%) over the next three years rather than another multi-bagger, which is why Jordi exited his Micron position despite still believing memory scarcity is real.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Apply a diminishing-forward-returns discipline after outsized gains rather than assuming the same multiple repeats.
insights.quote: If you're lucky enough ever in life to make a 7 to 10 bagger, don't expect there to be that much more going forward.

themes.theme: Enterprise AI adoption and the application layer

insights.claim: Scott Wu (Cognition AI CEO) frames AI progress as autonomous task duration expanding from seconds to hours of uninterrupted work, with the open question being whether this scales to days, weeks, months, or eventually a year of self-directed work; once agents can debug, search for context, and self-test over hours, they function as a 'junior coworker' rather than a tool.
insights.speaker: Scott Wu (quoted by host)
insights.so_what: Track autonomous-task-duration benchmarks as the key leading indicator of how much labor AI agents can realistically substitute for next.

insights.claim: Greg Brockman (OpenAI) states compute demand will structurally exceed supply indefinitely — every provider sells out all available compute even at only 'tens of millions' of agent users, a level the world has not yet reached.
insights.speaker: Greg Brockman (quoted by host)
insights.so_what: Compute-owning infrastructure names retain pricing power and scarcity value as long as demand keeps outrunning even aggressive capacity build-outs.
insights.quote: There's not just not going to be enough compute in the world to satisfy all the demand.

insights.claim: Enterprise AI adoption has moved from an early 2025 phase of 'we need AI, we're falling behind' to a current phase of 'show me the ROI,' with the binding constraint now on the supply side (compute and energy availability) rather than on enterprise demand.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: The narrative shift from adoption-risk to ROI-scrutiny is a maturing-market signal that should show up first in application-layer margins.

insights.claim: Enterprises are increasingly adopting 'AI workers' rather than 'AI tools,' implying direct labor substitution, productivity leverage, and organizational redesign rather than incremental efficiency gains.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: This reframing should show up as expanding profit margins at adopting companies well before it shows up in aggregate GDP or wage data.
insights.quote: Companies are not adopting AI tools. They are adopting AI workers.

insights.claim: Insurance (via the KBW Insurance Index and names like Travelers and All State) and healthcare administrative back-office work (in the mold of McKesson) are currently the two sectors showing the clearest AI-driven margin improvement, mainly by cutting paperwork and call-center costs.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Screen for 52-week-high breakouts in insurance and healthcare-admin names as a tangible, tradeable expression of the AI application-layer thesis today.

insights.claim: Regional bank consolidation is likely as larger banks that can afford to deploy AI internally acquire smaller banks that lack the budget to do so themselves, then rapidly cut costs at the acquired banks.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Watch KRE (regional bank ETF) constituents for M&A activity as an AI-driven consolidation trade distinct from the AI infrastructure trade itself.

insights.claim: AI-based medical diagnosis is already meaningfully impactful today and, according to the Greg Brockman interview, is expected to become the mainstream standard of care.
insights.speaker: Greg Brockman (quoted by host)
insights.so_what: AI-driven diagnostics adoption is a long-duration secular theme with near-term evidence already accumulating, not a speculative future case.

insights.claim: The story that Meta plans to resell excess AI compute capacity (SpaceX-style) is being misread as bearish oversupply; Meta's own Q1 earnings said it remains 'compute constrained' with demand outrunning expectations, and it signed a $27B cloud deal in March and a $21B expansion in April — the resale idea looks more like a stock-support narrative amid investor backlash than evidence of real oversupply.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Don't read Meta's compute-resale headline as a capex-cut signal; the underlying earnings commentary still points to structural compute scarcity.
insights.quote: We are still compute constrained... even as we aggressively add capacity, demand keeps outrunning our own expectations.

insights.claim: Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle ('the three bad boys') all show negative price-to-free-cash-flow dynamics relative to their capex spend, meaning capex is running ahead of visible monetization, which is fueling investor anxiety about AI ROI even as demand signals remain constrained.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Negative price-to-FCF at the hyperscalers is the mechanical reason for stock weakness even without a change in the underlying compute-demand thesis.

insights.claim: Google and Meta seeking additional capital (equity raises, cloud commitments) for AI capex is itself evidence that compute remains scarce, since neither would need to raise more money if they already had excess capacity.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Track hyperscaler capital-raising activity as a real-time indicator of compute scarcity, independent of guidance language.

insights.claim: Slower or smaller-than-expected hyperscaler capex guidance going forward is more likely explained by physical bottlenecks — insufficient data centers and gigawatts per SemiAnalysis and BIS data — than by falling AI demand.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Interpret any deceleration in hyperscaler capex growth rates as a supply-side physical constraint, not as a demand-cancellation signal for the AI trade.

themes.theme: Fed policy under Kevin Warsh and the debasement trade

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh's rhetoric sounds hawkish (rejecting forward guidance, saying the Fed 'should not spoon feed markets') but Jordi believes actual rate hikes are unlikely, because Warsh himself has acknowledged AI is both deflationary and job-displacing, undercutting the rationale to tighten.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Fade market pricing for Fed hikes; Warsh's own AI framework argues against tightening even as his public tone stays hawkish ahead of the midterms.
insights.quote: The Fed should not spoon feed markets.

insights.claim: Warsh has stated the improvement in AI models is happening at an exponential, 'hyper Moore's law' rate and suggested current business surveys may be underestimating how fast AI adoption will show up in the real economy.
insights.speaker: Kevin Warsh (quoted by host)
insights.so_what: A Fed governor publicly citing exponential AI adoption is itself informative context for how the Fed may weigh productivity and labor effects going forward.

insights.claim: Warsh prefers trimmed-inflation gauges (the Dallas Fed trimmed mean and Cleveland Fed median PCE) over the Fed's official core-PCE target, and the Cleveland Fed median PCE is only about 50 basis points above its post-Great-Financial-Crisis level even though the Fed funds rate is roughly 80 basis points more restrictive than it was in that era.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: On the inflation gauge the incoming Fed leadership actually favors, current policy already looks meaningfully restrictive, arguing against further hikes.

insights.claim: The Atlanta Fed median wage growth rate (about 3.5%) and the job quits rate both sit in the middle of their 2010-2020 range, showing no evidence of an overheating labor market; excluding healthcare, there is effectively no jobs market growth at all.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Wage and quits data undercut the 'tight labor market' justification some macro commentators use for expecting Fed hikes.
insights.quote: If you strip out health care there is no jobs market.

insights.claim: Positioning data (CFTC/COT and dollar-yen) shows the market has become crowded long the US dollar on the belief the Fed will hike three times; Jordi thinks this is wrong, and an unwind of that hike-pricing would be bullish for gold, silver, and Bitcoin together.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: A reversal in crowded long-dollar/short-precious-metals-and-crypto positioning is the trigger to watch for a debasement-trade rally.

insights.claim: Headline CPI is tracking toward negative monthly prints (June came in slightly below zero; the Cleveland Fed nowcasts July at about -0.2%), which could pull year-over-year CPI down from 4.2% toward roughly 3.5%, undercutting hawkish narratives built around core PCE.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Falling headline CPI prints are a near-term catalyst that could force a repricing of Fed-hike expectations lower.

insights.claim: The CBO's federal debt path implies roughly $7 trillion in annual net interest costs by 2036 under current Fed funds rate assumptions, forming the core structural bull case for gold, silver, and Bitcoin as debasement hedges — tempered only by the possibility that AI-driven disease cures could reduce future mandatory healthcare spending.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: The debt/interest trajectory is a multi-year structural tailwind for hard-asset and debasement-hedge positioning, independent of near-term Fed rhetoric.

insights.claim: Warsh noted it took 18 years to build the Fed's current large balance sheet and would take far more than 18 weeks to unwind it, signaling he intends to rely primarily on interest-rate policy rather than aggressive balance-sheet reduction (QT).
insights.speaker: Kevin Warsh (quoted by host)
insights.so_what: Expect rates, not an accelerated balance-sheet runoff, to remain the Fed's primary policy lever under Warsh.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's 60-day realized volatility is near its lowest level of the current cycle, and price has shown its first bullish divergence (lower price lows against higher momentum highs) since July of the prior year, a technical signal Jordi reads as a potential bottom forming.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: The realized-vol compression plus the first bullish price/momentum divergence in roughly a year is a concrete technical setup to watch for a Bitcoin low.

insights.claim: Over the last 100 trading days Bitcoin's rate-of-change has improved to about -5.8% (versus deeper prior losses), even though it remains below its 200-day moving average, showing the bear trend is decelerating.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: A decelerating rate-of-change to the downside often precedes a bottom even before price reclaims longer-term moving averages.

insights.claim: Widespread fear that Michael Saylor / Strategy could 'blow up' Bitcoin is read by Jordi as a contrarian bottom signal rather than a genuine risk, similar to prior points when maximum bearish sentiment coincided with lows.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Peak fear narratives around large concentrated holders are historically a sentiment-based buy signal in this framework, not a reason to sell.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's bottoming setup coincided with bad-news days — support for the Clarity Act falling to 39% and President Trump disclosing $1.4 billion in income — suggesting the market is absorbing negative headlines rather than being driven lower by them.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Price resilience on bad news is a classic exhaustion-of-selling signal worth confirming with follow-through price action.

insights.claim: Real two-year rates and inflation expectations began driving Bitcoin, gold, silver, and the dollar together since late March as the market priced in a more hawkish Fed; if the Fed stays on hold into the midterms as Jordi expects, this shared headwind should lift Bitcoin alongside gold and silver.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Monitor real 2-year rates and inflation swaps as the macro driver linking Bitcoin's price action to Fed policy expectations, not crypto-specific news.

insights.claim: Jordi has rotated the majority of his AI-infrastructure/semiconductor exposure out of memory names (having exited Micron after a large gain) and into gold/silver and Bitcoin-related names, while keeping Marvell as his largest remaining semiconductor position alongside a smaller stake in Fluence.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: The host's own rotation from AI-infrastructure/memory into precious metals and Bitcoin is a direct, actionable expression of his Fed/debasement thesis.

insights.claim: Jordi has also added Eli Lilly as a new position, separate from his AI-infrastructure book, reflecting a broader diversification of his thematic portfolio into healthcare alongside the AI trade.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Track healthcare/pharma additions as part of the same application-layer and AI-productivity thesis he applies to insurance and diagnostics.

insights.claim: Jordi now personally uses OpenAI's Codex more than Claude Code for his own workflow and has significantly scaled back his use of Gemini, Grok, and Perplexity, while recommending listeners use multiple AI coding tools rather than sticking to just one.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (host)
insights.so_what: Revealed-preference tool usage by a sophisticated practitioner is a real-world signal of relative coding-agent quality and market share shifts among AI tools.
